reason: Book states verbatim: 'If a module's interface is much simpler than its implementation, there will be many aspects of the module that can be changed without affecting other modules' (Ch4.4 Deep modules: 'best modules are those that provide powerful functionality yet have simple interfaces').

Ch3 'Working Code Isn't Enough (Strategic vs. Tactical Programming)' argues 'working code isn't enough... your primary goal must be to produce a great design' — strategic over tactical, matches claim.
Ch14 Choosing Names has subsections '14.3 Names should be precise' and '14.4 Use names consistently' — the two properties cited (precision, consistency) match the book's structure.
Book states verbatim: 'I am not a fan of test-driven development... This is tactical programming pure and simple, with all of its disadvantages' (Ch19.4).

Distorted example: Tcl's unset is used in section 10.2 'Too many exceptions' as a mistake/cautionary tale ('one of the biggest mistakes I made in the design of Tcl'), not as an illustration of the recommended 'define errors out of existence' technique in 10.3-10.5 (those use Windows file deletion and Java's substring method instead).
Book section 13.2 'Don't repeat the code' states 'the comments repeat the code... Comments like this are rarely useful' — matches claim directly.

No 'Three Dimensions in Tension' framework found anywhere in the book; Ch20 'Designing for Performance' subsections are 'How to think about performance', 'Measure before modifying', 'Design around the critical path', 'RAMCloud Buffers' — no performance/generality/safety triad exists.
Quote not found in book text (searched 'design your way out' and variants) — invented quote.

Stance inversion: book explicitly rejects TDD ('I am not a fan of test-driven development', Ch19.4) and instead recommends writing comments first as a design tool (Ch15 'Write The Comments First'), not tests first.
No 'three unrelated files' rule found anywhere in the book — invented specific numeric rule not supported by any grep match.
Book's example is '10.5 Example: Java substring method' (the Java String class), not Tcl — wrong language/example distorts the actual illustration.

No word-list of banned vague names like 'data, info, handle' found; the 'Red Flag: Vague Name' concept is illustrated instead with specific case examples (cursorVisible, VOTED_FOR_SENTINEL_VALUE, result), not a banned-terms list.
